main_summary.summary: Nathaniel "Nate" Hawthorne Jones (born 1914, Claiborne County, Mississippi) was interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 3, 2015, in his home on land where he was born. Jones recounts growing up sharecropping with his father and stepmother in a family of fifteen children, attending a six-month school in Egypt, Mississippi, where Rev. Martin's wife taught and where Jones first encountered Rev. Martin's NAACP organizing. Drafted into the Navy in 1944, Jones trained at Camp Shelby and Bainbridge, served as a steward and cook in the Pacific theater (Saipan, Majuro, Eniwetok, Hawaii), and participated in a hunger strike to integrate the officers' mess. He joined the underground NAACP in 1952 through Mr. Charlie Cook and Rev. Martin, meeting in secret at the Egypt Methodist church. Using Navy pay sent home to his future wife, Jones bought 126 acres after the war. He registered to vote alongside Mr. Ellis Collins and Mr. Griffin under poll-tax and literacy-test conditions, attended Bob Moses's voter-registration class during R.L.T. Smith's 1962 congressional campaign, and became an NAACP officer when Charles Evers, Rudy Shields, George Henry Walker, and Ms. Leesco Guster brought the movement to Claiborne County. Jones led tear-gassed students back to the dormitory during the 1966 Alcorn protest, was arrested in the march, helped found the A-Mart cooperative grocery, and was a named defendant in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware (won 1982). His wife was elected to county office in 1971.
key_themes: Underground NAACP organizing in 1950s Claiborne County
Navy service and Pacific theater segregation
Voter registration under poll tax and literacy tests
Claiborne County movement, boycott, and Claiborne Hardware lawsuit
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jones provides one of very few first-person accounts of underground NAACP membership in 1950s rural Mississippi, including the operational mechanics of secret Saturday-afternoon meetings at the Egypt Methodist church, the need to hide membership cards, and the role of trusted local clergy like Rev. Martin as recruitment intermediaries. His testimony layers Pacific-theater military service (Camp Shelby, Bainbridge, Hawaii, Majuro, Saipan, Eniwetok) and a Navy hunger strike for mess integration onto the longer Claiborne County movement arc, providing continuity between Black WWII veteran activism and the post-1965 organizing wave.

The interview is a primary source for the Claiborne County voter-registration effort, the 1962 R.L.T. Smith congressional campaign with Bob Moses's voter-registration class, the 1966 Alcorn College tear-gas protest (including the specific role of patrolmen and the beating of June Warners's uncle), the A-Mart cooperative grocery store founded with R.L.T. Smith and Alexander Collins as shareholders, and the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware lawsuit (1982 NAACP victory). Jones's account of his wife's 1971 election to county office, his retention of paternalistic white-banker friendships (Mr. Eli Ellis) alongside open NAACP membership, and his work as a school-bus driver hired by superintendent Robert Seders documents the cross-cutting white-Black relational landscape that the published literature rarely captures with this granularity.

chapters.title: Birth and Sharecropping Childhood
chapters.summary: Jones describes being born in 1914 on the Claiborne County land where the interview is conducted, the family's move from Lorman, Mississippi, and growing up as the first of his father's ten children in a sharecropping household of fifteen after his mother died and his father remarried. He details the family's obligation to raise cotton, corn, and peas under a one-fourth share arrangement, with the landowner taking a portion of every crop.

chapters.title: School at Egypt and Alcorn
chapters.summary: Jones recounts attending a six-month school at the Egypt Methodist church where Rev. Martin's wife taught, having to leave by age nine to work the fields, and keeping up by studying assignments his younger sister Emma brought home. He describes traveling to Alcorn (College) commencements to watch baseball and his early subscription to the Pittsburgh Courier, which his future wife later mailed to him during Navy service.

chapters.title: Underground NAACP, 1952
chapters.summary: Jones describes joining the underground NAACP around 1952 through his friend Mr. Charlie Cook, who connected him with Rev. Martin of the Egypt Methodist church. Members met covertly on Saturday afternoons when the streets were full, kept their membership cards hidden because the Klan was active, and faced retaliation if discovered. Jones recalls hearing about freedom and the Mason-Dixon Line and wanting to join despite the danger.

chapters.title: Navy Service in the Pacific
chapters.summary: Drafted in 1944, Jones trained at Camp Shelby and Bainbridge before deploying via San Diego and Hawaii to the Pacific. As a steward and cook he served at Majuro, near Saipan and Eniwetok, taking shelter in lagoons during air and ship raids. Jones describes participating in a three-day hunger strike of African American stewards demanding to eat with the officers, which succeeded.

chapters.title: Voter Registration and R.L.T. Smith
chapters.summary: Jones recounts going with Mr. Ellis Collins and Mr. Griffin to register to vote, being given a section of the Mississippi Constitution to copy and interpret, and being failed twice before passing. He describes Rev. R.L.T. Smith's congressional campaign and attending a voter-registration class led by a young New Yorker, Bob Moses, after which police waited outside but had been deterred from beating the participants.

chapters.title: Marriage and Buying the Land
chapters.summary: Jones describes meeting his future wife when she came to help his brother's wife with their first child, and marrying after he returned from the Navy. He details sending his $100 monthly Navy pay home to her, saving toward land purchase, and after discharge buying 126 acres from a seller named Mr. Mehaw for approximately $1,400.

chapters.title: Movement Comes to Claiborne County
chapters.summary: Jones describes how the local movement gained momentum when Charles Evers, Rudy Shields, George Henry Walker, and Ms. Leesco Guster came to Claiborne County to organize voter registration on the plantations. He explains the white establishment's formation of a biracial committee of preachers and teachers chosen to slow the movement, and the Black community's resistance to that containment strategy.

chapters.title: NAACP Officer and Robert Seders
chapters.summary: When the NAACP needed officers Jones accepted the role openly. He describes the white-Black relationships that supported him, including superintendent Robert Seders who sat in Jones's kitchen, hired his wife to teach in the school system, and hired him to drive a school bus. Mr. Eli Ellis, the vice-president at the bank, confronted Jones about joining the NAACP but had been good to him on loans and hunting visits.

chapters.title: 1966 Alcorn Tear-Gas Protest
chapters.summary: Jones recounts the 1966 protest at Alcorn College over student treatment and wage issues for campus workers. He describes leading students back to the dormitory through paths he knew from hauling logs by horse, the patrolmen throwing tear gas, and the beating of June Warners's uncle and a preacher from Hattiesburg. Jones was arrested with the marchers and jailed overnight while his wife took over his school-bus route.

chapters.title: Boycott, Marches, and Mass Meetings
chapters.summary: Jones describes mass meetings with clapping and singing of "We Shall Overcome" and "Freedom," closing marches around town, and the Port Gibson boycott focused on worker pay and conditions. He explains how many residents were afraid to participate because plantation owners controlled their housing and would evict families whose children joined, and describes the local Black-head youth group that threw rocks until adult leadership broke them up.

chapters.title: Black Candidates and Wife's Election
chapters.summary: Jones describes the difficulty of finding Black candidates willing to risk their jobs in the 1967 Claiborne County elections, recruiting Mr. Ellis Collins among the first three. He recounts his wife's 1971 election to circuit court office, his pride at her victory, and the support he received from white friends including the school superintendent and his banker.

chapters.title: A-Mart Cooperative and Claiborne Hardware
chapters.summary: Jones describes founding the A-Mart cooperative grocery store around 1967 with twenty-five-dollar shares, with Rev. R.L.T. Smith as the leading shareholder, Alexander Collins second, and the Joneses third. He recounts the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware lawsuit alleging the boycott had cut white merchants' profits, which the NAACP won in 1982 after years of litigation that tied up his property without preventing him from farming with the help of white friends.

chapters.title: Reflections and Family Logging
chapters.summary: Jones closes with reflections on the difficulty of carrying NAACP cards in his early years (after hearing of a young man killed for having one), the importance of his organizational training, and his pride at participating openly once it became possible. He describes his large family working together logging and picking cotton on plantations, often four members harvesting over two hundred pounds in a day.

loc_video.caption: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Claiborne County, Mississippi, 2015 December 03
